nassau.name: Fort Nassau
nassau.note: Burned by Franco-Spanish raiders in 1703; the pirates never repaired it. Woodes Rogers remounted its 28 guns in 1718 with pardoned pirate labour.

nassau.name: The Careening Beach, Hog Island
nassau.note: Where sloops were hove down for cleaning — a pirate ship's life depended on a clean bottom, and Nassau's beach was always busy.

nassau.name: Eastern Anchorage
nassau.note: Too shallow for men-of-war: the harbour's whole strategic value. Vane fled past Rogers here behind a fireship, July 1718.

nassau.name: The Western Bar
nassau.note: The deep-draught entrance, commanded by the fort — when the fort had powder.

nassau.name: The Tent Camps
nassau.note: Most of the thousand-odd pirates lived ashore under sailcloth and palmetto, drinking the proceeds. Rum, when plentiful, sold cheaper than water.

nassau.name: Vendue House quay
nassau.note: Where 'wrack' goods and pirate plunder alike were auctioned — questions discouraged.

nassau.name: The Gallows
nassau.note: Eight of Vane's diehards hanged here in December 1718 while the crowd watched in silence — the end of the Republic of Pirates.

port-royal.name: The Sunken City
port-royal.note: Two-thirds of buccaneer Port Royal slid into the harbour in the earthquake of 7 June 1692; pipes, pewter and whole streets still lie in 10 m of water.

port-royal.name: Fort Charles
port-royal.note: The surviving fort of the old five; headquarters of the Royal Navy's Jamaica station in 1730. A young Nelson will pace its ramparts in '79.

port-royal.name: Gallows Point
port-royal.note: Calico Jack Rackham hanged here Nov 1720; Charles Vane in 1721. Rackham's tarred body was gibbeted on the cay now called Rackham's Cay.

port-royal.name: Chocolata Hole
port-royal.note: The careening cove for navy ships and merchantmen — the navy's only careening place east of Antigua.

port-royal.name: The Wherry anchorage
port-royal.note: Deep water right against the town: ships once tied to the houses. Half of it is now over the drowned streets.

port-royal.name: Kingston (new town)
port-royal.note: Founded for the earthquake refugees of 1692 on a healthy plain; by 1730 it has taken Port Royal's trade, if not its navy.

port-royal.name: Mosquito Point battery
port-royal.note: Crossfire over the harbour channel with Fort Charles.

tortuga.name: Basse-Terre roadstead
tortuga.note: The buccaneers' anchorage of the 1640s–80s, facing the Hispaniola hunting grounds across the channel.

tortuga.name: Fort de Rocher
tortuga.note: Le Vasseur's rock fortress (1640s) with its 'dovecote' keep, reached by an iron ladder drawn up at need — it beat off a Spanish fleet in 1643.

tortuga.name: La Montagne lookouts
tortuga.note: From the 460 m ridge, lookouts could mark sails a day before they reached the channel.

tortuga.name: Cayonne
tortuga.note: The old buccaneer town: taverns, boucans and the French governor's pretensions.

ile-sainte-marie.name: Baie des Forbans (Pirates' Bay)
ile-sainte-marie.note: The hidden anchorage: reef outside, friendly Betsimisaraka ashore, and the Mocha route to windward. Twenty ships have lain here at once.

ile-sainte-marie.name: Îlot Madame
ile-sainte-marie.note: Adam Baldridge's fortified trading post (1691–97) fenced pirate plunder to New York merchants — rum and powder in, eight-reales and silks out.

ile-sainte-marie.name: The Pirate Cemetery
ile-sainte-marie.note: Skull-carved headstones above the bay; legend (wrongly) puts Kidd here. Real graves of the Round's dead from fever, drink and each other.

ile-sainte-marie.name: Wreck of the Adventure Galley
ile-sainte-marie.note: Kidd scuttled his rotten galley here in 1698; her ballast lies under the harbour mud.

ile-sainte-marie.name: Wreck of the Fiery Dragon
ile-sainte-marie.note: Burned 1721 with porcelain and gold dust aboard — excavated three centuries later.

ile-sainte-marie.name: Careening cove
ile-sainte-marie.note: Hulls grew foul fast in tropical water; the Round's ships hove down here between cruises.

ile-sainte-marie.name: Ratsimilaho's landing
ile-sainte-marie.note: The half-English son of pirate Tom White united the coast as king of the Betsimisaraka — pirate blood turned royalty.

bath-ocracoke.name: Teach's Hole
bath-ocracoke.note: Blackbeard's anchorage inside Ocracoke Inlet — and the spot where Maynard's sloops found him at dawn, 22 Nov 1718.

bath-ocracoke.name: Springer's Point
bath-ocracoke.note: The pirates' watering place under the live oaks; site of the great pirate banyan of October 1718 with Vane and Rackham.

bath-ocracoke.name: Bath Town
bath-ocracoke.note: North Carolina's tiny capital, where Teach took the King's pardon from Gov. Eden in June 1718 and married his fourteenth wife.

bath-ocracoke.name: Plum Point (Teach's house)
bath-ocracoke.note: The point opposite Bath where Blackbeard kept a house — convenient for watching who came up the creek, and for Governor Eden's back door, gossips said.

havana.name: El Morro
havana.note: The castle on the harbour throat; its chain boom closed the port each night. The light burned for the plate fleets.

havana.name: La Punta
havana.note: El Morro's partner across the channel — the boom ran between them.

havana.name: Castillo de la Real Fuerza
havana.note: The old fort within the town, treasury of fleets in transit; the Giraldilla weathervane above it is Havana's emblem.

havana.name: Royal Shipyard (Arsenal)
havana.note: Building the biggest warships in the Americas from Cuban cedar and mahogany — cheaper and longer-lived than Spanish oak.

havana.name: Plate Fleet anchorage
havana.note: Where the combined flotas assembled for the run home through the Florida Strait, deep enough for a hundred sail.

havana.name: The Contaduría quay
havana.note: Silver weighed, registered and (some of it) taxed before lading; the difference between manifest and hold was Havana's open secret.

cartagena.name: Boca Chica forts
cartagena.note: The only deep entrance to the bay, gated by San Luis and its batteries — Pointis forced it in 1697; Vernon will in 1741, briefly.

cartagena.name: Castillo San Felipe de Barajas
cartagena.note: The hill-castle commanding the land approach; the strongest single work in the Americas.

cartagena.name: The Walls & Las Bóvedas
cartagena.note: Full circuit of ramparts rebuilt after the 1697 sack; the vaults beneath serve as magazines.

cartagena.name: Galeones anchorage
cartagena.note: The treasure fleet lay here for months while the Portobelo fair turned Peru's silver into Europe's goods.

cartagena.name: Slave landing (Casa de la Aduana)
cartagena.note: The English asiento's factors land their human cargo here under the customs house arches.

cartagena.name: Wager's Action, 1708
cartagena.note: Seaward of Barú, Commodore Wager met the galeones at dusk; the San José exploded with the fair's silver and ~600 men.

portobelo.name: The Iron Castle (San Felipe)
portobelo.note: Guarding the bay mouth; Morgan stormed it in 1668 using captured monks as a human shield, the story goes.

portobelo.name: Santiago & San Jerónimo
portobelo.note: The inner forts ringing the anchorage — rebuilt after every sack, stormed anyway.

portobelo.name: The Customs House (Aduana)
portobelo.note: During the fair, silver bars were stacked in the plaza like firewood for want of room — eyewitnesses swore to it.

portobelo.name: Mule road to Panamá
portobelo.note: The Camino Real: Peru's silver crossed the isthmus on mule trains — the prize Drake and Morgan came for.

portobelo.name: The fever roads
portobelo.note: Hosier's blockade squadron anchored here in 1726 and buried 4,000 men without a battle — 'Hosier's Ghost' became a ballad of complaint.

london.name: Execution Dock, Wapping
london.note: Pirates hanged at the low-tide mark with a shortened drop, then chained until three tides washed over them. Kidd's rope broke in 1701; they hanged him again.

london.name: The Pool of London
london.note: A forest of masts from the Bridge to Wapping; the richest mile of water in the world.

london.name: Deptford Royal Dockyard
london.note: The navy's building yard since Henry VIII; East Indiamen fit out at Blackwall across the river.

london.name: Lloyd's Coffee House
london.note: Where ships, cargoes and the lives of sailors are insured — and where pirate news arrives first, priced into the premiums.

london.name: The Tower & Trinity House
london.note: Ordnance for the fleets; pilots and lights for the Thames.

boston.name: Long Wharf
boston.note: Half a mile into the harbour (1711), the largest wharf in the English world — ships unload straight into King Street warehouses.

boston.name: Castle William
boston.note: The harbour fort on Castle Island, rebuilt in stone after 1701.

boston.name: Nix's Mate
boston.note: The gibbet islet: pirate William Fly's bones hung here in chains from 1726 as the harbour's welcome sign. The sea is eating the island — sailors call it judgement.

boston.name: North End shipyards
boston.note: Launching more tonnage than all other American ports together; a ship a fortnight in good years.

boston.name: The Great Brewhouse & ropewalks
boston.note: Rope, beer and Bibles — Boston's idea of ship's stores.

charleston.name: White Point
charleston.note: Stede Bonnet and 29 of his men hanged here below the high-water mark in Nov–Dec 1718, then buried in the marsh.

charleston.name: Granville Bastion
charleston.note: Southeast corner of the walled town, guns over the Cooper River.

charleston.name: Rebellion Road & the Bar
charleston.note: Ships cross the bar with the tide and a pilot — here Blackbeard's squadron lay in May 1718, taking every ship that tried.

charleston.name: Sullivan's Island lookout
charleston.note: The pest-house and lookout island; signal fires warn the town of sails — or pirates.

cadiz.name: San Sebastián castle
cadiz.note: On its islet at the city's seaward horn; with Santa Catalina it crosses fire over the approaches.

cadiz.name: Puntales narrows
cadiz.note: The inner bay's bottleneck where the flotas lie; Essex burned a fleet here in 1596 — never forgotten, never repeated.

cadiz.name: Casa de Contratación
cadiz.note: Since 1717 the House of Trade rules here: registries, licences, pilots' charts and the silver ledgers of the Indies.

cadiz.name: La Carraca arsenal
cadiz.note: The new naval arsenal rising in the marshes — Spain rebuilding her sea power under Patiño.

veracruz.name: San Juan de Ulúa
veracruz.note: The island fortress: ships moor to bronze rings set in its walls, safe from northers and pirates alike.

veracruz.name: The mole anchorage
veracruz.note: Between fort and town; the flota discharges into lighters here for the Mexico City road.

veracruz.name: Gates to the Camino Real
veracruz.note: The silver road inland: mule trains to Jalapa, Puebla and the mint of Mexico.

bridgetown.name: Carlisle Bay
bridgetown.note: The open roadstead where the sugar fleets load; hurricanes have twice piled it with wrecks.

bridgetown.name: Needham's Point fort
bridgetown.note: James Fort's guns cover the bay's southern horn.

bridgetown.name: The Careenage
bridgetown.note: The inner creek where island sloops heave down between voyages.

bridgetown.name: The Cage & wharves
bridgetown.note: Sugar out, everything in — including, in season, the slave sales that feed the whole machine.

cape-town.name: Castle of Good Hope
cape-town.note: The VOC's five-bastioned castle (1679), seat of the Governor and the Council of Policy.

cape-town.name: Table Bay roadstead
cape-town.note: Holding ground is poor and the northwest gales of winter deadly — whole fleets have gone ashore. The Company forbids winter calls after 1722, then relents.

cape-town.name: Company's Garden
cape-town.note: The vegetable garden that founded a colony: greens and water for scurvy-ridden fleets.

cape-town.name: Robben Island
cape-town.note: Sheep, lime-burning and exiles — the Company's prison island already.

cape-town.name: False Bay (Simon's Bay)
cape-town.note: The winter anchorage; pirates on the Round watered here too, flying false colours.

batavia.name: The Kasteel
batavia.note: The VOC's four-bastion castle at the river mouth: the Governor-General, the spice warehouses, and the gallows yard.

batavia.name: Batavia roads
batavia.note: Ships anchor among the islands offshore and lighter their cargo up the canal; the roads hold sixty Indiamen in season.

batavia.name: Onrust Island yard
batavia.note: The careening and repair island — 'Unrest' by name, working day and night when the return fleet fits out.

batavia.name: The walled city & canals
batavia.note: Amsterdam in the tropics: gabled houses, fetid canals, and fevers that earn it the name 'graveyard of Europeans'.

manila.name: Fort Santiago & Intramuros
manila.note: The walled Spanish city at the Pasig mouth; the galleon's silver registers pass through these gates.

manila.name: Cavite arsenal
manila.note: Across the bay: the galleons — the largest ships in the world — are built and refitted here of Philippine teak and lanang.

manila.name: Manila Bay anchorage
manila.note: The Acapulco galleon and the Amoy junks share the roadstead each season; everything China sells, for everything Mexico mints.

manila.name: Corregidor watch
manila.note: The island at the bay mouth where lookouts first cry the galleon's return — or an enemy's sail.

acapulco.name: Fort San Diego
acapulco.note: Star-shaped walls over the anchorage, rebuilt after the 1715 earthquake; the galleon's silver sleeps here before the fair.

acapulco.name: Galleon anchorage
acapulco.note: Deep, landlocked, hurricane-safe — the finest natural harbour on the coast, empty ten months a year.

acapulco.name: The Fair grounds
acapulco.note: When the galleon arrives (December–January), merchants from Mexico City triple the town: Chinese silks against Potosí silver, then silence again.
